summary:
The First Fleet (1787–1788 AD) was the flotilla of eleven ships that transported the first European settlers to [[event:2464|Australia]] — 736 convicts (including 192 women), 252 marines and their families, and the colony's governor and officials — sailing from Portsmouth on May 13, 1787 under Captain Arthur Phillip and arriving at Botany Bay on January 18–20, 1788. The fleet's journey of approximately 24,000 kilometers via the Canary Islands, Rio de Janeiro, and the Cape of Good Hope took eight months, during which 48 people died. On January 26, 1788 (now celebrated as Australia Day), Governor Phillip raised the British flag at Sydney Cove — a small harbor in Port Jackson that he had selected as superior to Botany Bay — formally claiming the territory.

The First Fleet's arrival was the beginning of an irreversible transformation of the Australian continent. The 3,000-4,000 years of accumulated knowledge of the Eora people who inhabited the Sydney region were rendered irrelevant almost overnight as European settlement brought disease, displacement, and cultural destruction. The fleet carried smallpox inoculation materials, but smallpox nevertheless devastated Aboriginal coastal populations within months of arrival — possibly from earlier Dutch contact, possibly from the fleet itself. Governor Phillip's initial instructions were to establish friendly relations with Indigenous people, and he made some genuine attempts at contact, but the structural dynamics of colonial settlement made conflict inevitable. The colony struggled in its first years with food shortages, insubordinate convicts, and the difficulty of building an agricultural economy in an unfamiliar environment, but by 1800 had established a precarious but permanent European presence.
